proposals.title: Single-Threaded LINEITEM GROUP BY Subquery — subquery_scan Dominance
proposals.detect: subquery_scan phase > 60% of hot runtime in a query that computes a GROUP BY + HAVING aggregate over LINEITEM (the largest table, ~60M rows at SF10). Pattern: SELECT l_orderkey FROM lineitem GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM(l_quantity) > threshold.
proposals.impact: Q18 final: subquery_scan 451 ms / 512 ms hot = 88% of runtime. Persisted across all 5 optimization iterations because the optimizer targeted join-probe phases (orders_probe, customer_probe) which were already <10 ms.
proposals.fix: Parallelize the subquery GROUP BY scan with thread-local partial hash maps and a merge phase. Pattern: (1) partition lineitem rows across N threads by row range; (2) each thread accumulates thread-local unordered_map<int32_t, double> for l_orderkey -> SUM(l_quantity); (3) merge into a single hash set of qualifying orderkeys; (4) probe orders/customer with that set. At SF10 with 8 threads: estimated subquery_scan < 60 ms (7x reduction from 451 ms). Also applicable to any single-table aggregation subquery where the table has >10M rows.

proposals.title: Optimizer Metric Mismatch — Total Timing Selects Wrong Best for Hot Workloads
proposals.detect: hot_timing_ms of the selected best-known source is significantly worse than another passing candidate's hot_timing_ms, because the selection criterion uses timing_ms (cold+hot combined). Pattern: iter_A has (cold=X, hot=Y) where X+Y < iter_B's cold+hot, but iter_B.hot < iter_A.hot — and iter_A is chosen as best.
proposals.impact: Q18: iter_4 selected as best (total 2,787 ms) over iter_5 (total 2,976 ms), even though iter_5 has hot=534 ms vs iter_4 hot=1,161 ms. A repeated warm-cache workload pays 1,161 ms per execution instead of 534 ms. The 'best' code is 2.2x slower in practice.
proposals.fix: Change best-selection metric to hot_timing_ms as the PRIMARY criterion, with total timing_ms as a secondary tiebreaker. For an OLAP engine, the warm (cached) execution time is what matters after the first call. Only prefer lower total timing when hot timings are within 10% of each other.

proposals.title: Bottleneck-Phase Persistence — Optimizer Ignores the Dominant Phase
proposals.detect: The same phase name accounts for >70% of hot runtime across ALL optimization iterations without decreasing. Example: Q18 subquery_scan was 7,194 ms (iter_1) → 451 ms (iter_5) = improved, but in iter_4 and iter_5 it remains the dominant phase (88%+) while the optimizer's improvement efforts targeted other phases (orders_probe was reduced from 10 ms to 10 ms — already negligible).
proposals.impact: Optimizer wastes iterations on sub-dominant phases (<5% of runtime) while the >70% bottleneck phase is not directly targeted. Result: no further gain possible from non-bottleneck optimizations.
proposals.fix: After each iteration, compute phase_share = phase_time / total_hot_time for all phases. If max(phase_share) > 0.60 for the same phase across 2 consecutive iterations, force the next optimization prompt to explicitly state: 'The dominant bottleneck is <phase_name> at X% of hot runtime. Focus exclusively on parallelizing or restructuring this phase. Do not modify other phases.' This prevents optimizer drift toward already-optimized phases.

proposals.title: optimization_history / execution_results.json Timing Discrepancy — Stale Record
proposals.detect: optimization_history entry for iter_N shows a hot_timing_ms that differs by >10x from the corresponding iter_N/execution_results.json hot_timing_ms. Observed: Q18 optimization_history iter_1 records hot=145 ms, but iter_1/execution_results.json records hot=7,383 ms.
proposals.impact: The optimizer may make incorrect revert/advance decisions based on stale or misaligned timing data. A candidate that genuinely performed poorly may be incorrectly recorded as the best.
proposals.fix: When writing optimization_history entries, always cross-check timing_ms against the execution_results.json file for that iteration. The canonical truth is execution_results.json. If they diverge by >10%, log a warning and re-run the benchmark before recording the improvement decision.

proposals.title: date_utils.h Format-Overflow Warning — Buffer Too Small for Edge Year Values
proposals.detect: GCC warning: `'%02d' directive writing between 2 and 4 bytes into a region of size between 0 and 4` at date_utils.h:52. The sprintf buffer is sized 12 bytes but the formatted output can reach 17 bytes (e.g., '-9999-12-31'). Occurs in any query that calls epoch_days_to_date_str().
proposals.impact: Non-fatal for TPC-H dates (all within 1992–1998, well within buffer). Produces noisy compiler output that masks real warnings. Will become a buffer overflow if used with large negative epoch day values.
proposals.fix: In date_utils.h line 52, increase the char buf[] declaration from `char buf[12]` (or similar) to `char buf[20]` to accommodate the full ISO date range including sign and century digits. Alternatively use snprintf with the larger buffer size. Fixes the warning cleanly without behavioral change for TPC-H date ranges.
